screens.prompt: Case breakthrough: the burst is almost certainly a digitally modulated transmission — bits clocked out at a fixed symbol rate onto a carrier. If you estimate its mean and autocorrelation using a plain time-average (treating it as if it were an ordinary WSS process), do you expect those quantities to come out constant in time, or to visibly pulse with the symbol clock?
choices: Constant — modulated signals still look wide-sense stationary once you average enough
Pulsing — the act of clocking symbols out at a fixed rate makes the *statistics themselves* periodic in time
screens.reveal: They pulse. A digitally modulated signal is built by multiplying (or convolving) a random bit/symbol sequence against a periodic pulse shape and carrier, clocked at a fixed rate $1/T_0$. That periodic clocking doesn't just repeat in the raw waveform — it repeats in the waveform's **statistics**: $R_x(t,\tau) = E[x(t)x^*(t-\tau)]$ is periodic in $t$ with period $T_0$, not constant. A process whose statistics are periodic in time like this, rather than constant, is called **cyclostationary** — the formal generalization of stationarity you've been building toward since the random-processes module.

screens.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes a cyclostationary process from a wide-sense stationary one?

choices.text: WSS: R_x(t,τ) depends only on τ. Cyclostationary: R_x(t,τ) is periodic in t (for fixed τ), not constant.
WSS processes have zero mean; cyclostationary processes don't
choices.feedback: Mean value has nothing to do with the distinction — both classes can have zero or nonzero mean. The distinction is entirely about how R_x(t,τ) behaves as a function of t.

choices.text: Cyclostationary processes are just WSS processes measured over a longer window
choices.feedback: It isn't a measurement-window issue — no amount of averaging removes the periodicity in t for a genuinely cyclostationary process, because the periodicity is a property of the underlying random mechanism (the symbol clock), not an artifact of a short recording.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — cyclostationarity is a strict generalization of stationarity: WSS is the special case where the period in t happens to be irrelevant (constant statistics), and cyclostationary is the case where it isn't.
